content.problemStatement:
Raj is a math pro and number theory expert. One day, he met his age-old friend Chef. Chef claimed to be better at number theory than Raj, so Raj gave him some fuzzy problems to solve. In one of those problems, he gave Chef a 333-tuple of non-negative integers (a0,b0,c0)(a_0, b_0, c_0)(a0‚Äã,b0‚Äã,c0‚Äã) and told Chef to convert it to another tuple (x,y,z)(x, y, z)(x,y,z).

Chef may perform the following operations any number of times (including zero) on his current tuple (a,b,c)(a, b, c)(a,b,c), in any order:


Choose one element of this tuple, i.e. aaa, bbb or ccc. Either add 111 to that element or subtract 111 from it. The cost of this operation is 111.
Merge: Change the tuple to (a‚àí1,b‚àí1,c+1)(a-1, b-1, c+1)(a‚àí1,b‚àí1,c+1), (a‚àí1,b+1,c‚àí1)(a-1, b+1, c-1)(a‚àí1,b+1,c‚àí1) or (a+1,b‚àí1,c‚àí1)(a+1, b-1, c-1)(a+1,b‚àí1,c‚àí1), i.e. add 111 to one element and subtract 111 from the other two. The cost of this operation is 000.
Split: Change the tuple to (a‚àí1,b+1,c+1)(a-1, b+1, c+1)(a‚àí1,b+1,c+1), (a+1,b‚àí1,c+1)(a+1, b-1, c+1)(a+1,b‚àí1,c+1) or (a+1,b+1,c‚àí1)(a+1, b+1, c-1)(a+1,b+1,c‚àí1), i.e. subtract 111 from one element and add 111 to the other two. The cost of this operation is also 000.
After each operation, all elements of Chef's tuple must be non-negative. It is not allowed to perform an operation that would make one or more elements of this tuple negative.

Can you help Chef find the minimum cost of converting the tuple (a0,b0,c0)(a_0, b_0, c_0)(a0‚Äã,b0‚Äã,c0‚Äã) to the tuple (x,y,z)(x, y, z)(x,y,z)? It can be easily proved that it is always possible to convert any tuple of non-negative integers to any other.
constraints: Subtasks**Subtask #1 (20 points):** 0‚â§a0,b0,c0,x,y,z‚â§1000 \le a_0, b_0, c_0, x, y, z \le 1000‚â§a0‚Äã,b0‚Äã,c0‚Äã,x,y,z‚â§100
content.inputFormat:
The first line of the input contains a single integer TTT denoting the number of test cases. The description of TTT test cases follows.
The first and only line of each test case contains six space-separated integers a0a_0a0‚Äã, b0b_0b0‚Äã, c0c_0c0‚Äã, xxx, yyy and zzz.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, print a single line containing one integer ‚Äï the minimum cost.

sampleTestCases.explanation:
Explanation:
Example case 1: The tuple (1,1,1)(1, 1, 1)(1,1,1) can be converted to (2,2,2)(2, 2, 2)(2,2,2) using only three Split operations, with cost 000: (1,1,1)‚Üí(2,0,2)‚Üí(1,1,3)‚Üí(2,2,2)(1, 1, 1) \rightarrow (2, 0, 2) \rightarrow (1, 1, 3) \rightarrow (2, 2, 2)(1,1,1)‚Üí(2,0,2)‚Üí(1,1,3)‚Üí(2,2,2).
Example case 2: We can use one addition operation and one Split operation: (1,2,3)‚Üí(1,3,3)‚Üí(2,4,2)(1, 2, 3) \rightarrow (1, 3, 3) \rightarrow (2, 4, 2)(1,2,3)‚Üí(1,3,3)‚Üí(2,4,2).
